name: Fidelity National Information Services, Inc.

profile.description: Fidelity National Information Services is a global fintech company providing banking, capital markets, and issuer solutions to financial institutions worldwide. Following its January 2026 sale of Worldpay and acquisition of Global Payments' Issuer Solutions business, FIS operates as a pure-play banking and payments technology provider serving more than 8,500 financial institutions across 120+ countries.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): FIS completed a transformative corporate restructuring in January 2026, divesting Worldpay and simultaneously acquiring Global Payments' Issuer Solutions business to reshape itself as a pure-play banking and payments technology provider [2]. This is a significant strategic pivot that fundamentally altered the company's revenue composition and competitive positioning, though it represents portfolio restructuring rather than organic product innovation. AI Deployment (2/2): FIS has shipped specific AI products to customers — the Insurance Risk Suite AI Assistant launched in February 2026 provides 24/7 generative AI-powered risk modeling for insurance clients, and Treasury GPT, developed in partnership with Microsoft and OpenAI, embeds conversational AI directly into FIS's Treasury and Risk Manager platform [3]. These are revenue-generating AI products, not just internal efficiency tools. Market Share (2/2): FIS is the #1 core banking technology provider globally, serving 8,500+ financial institutions across 120+ countries with mission-critical software that processes trillions of dollars in transactions annually [1]. This dominant market position is reinforced by multi-year contracts, regulatory compliance requirements, and extremely high switching costs. Pipeline (1/2): The Issuer Solutions integration is creating a 'Total Issuing' platform combining debit, credit, and prepaid processing capabilities, and cloud modernization of the core banking stack continues. However, specific next-generation product timelines and revenue targets have not been clearly articulated beyond general AI and cloud themes. R&D Intensity (1/1): As a major fintech platform company, FIS invests significantly in technology development across banking, capital markets, and issuer solutions, consistent with its technology-first repositioning.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): FIS's purpose of 'Advancing the way the world pays, banks and invests' is clear, relevant, and identifies the company's domain, but it reads as a business description rather than an inspiring vision of a future state that doesn't yet exist [2]. It could apply to virtually any large fintech company. Community Impact (1/2): FIS publishes an annual ESG report and maintains community investment programs, but no specific dollar amounts, distinctive social programs, or measurable community outcomes were surfaced in research. The company's community engagement appears standard for a large-cap technology firm without notable differentiation. Employee Retention (0/2): Glassdoor ratings of 3.4/5 with recurring complaints about 'constant RIFs every three months,' merit increases described as 'non-existent or insultingly small,' and general dissatisfaction with organizational stability suggest a below-average employee experience [4]. Frequent restructuring cycles erode the trust and continuity needed for retention. Insider Buying (2/2): CEO Stephanie Ferris made a substantial open-market purchase of 19,846 shares at $50.39 per share (~$1M out-of-pocket) in March 2026, representing meaningful personal conviction [3]. Three insiders made open-market purchases in the last 6 months with only one sale, a strong insider buying signal.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. FIS trades at a deeply discounted Forward P/E of 6.03x with a PEG ratio of just 0.46, reflecting extreme market skepticism about the post-restructuring trajectory [4]. ROE of 17.22% exceeds the 15% target, and profit margin of 23.35% comfortably clears the 20% threshold, demonstrating strong profitability. D/E of 132% is elevated above the 70% target but below the more concerning levels seen at some leveraged peers. The dividend profile is mixed — a 4.56% yield and conservative 32.80% payout ratio are strengths, and 21 consecutive years of payments provide history, but the dividend was cut significantly in 2023 and has only 2 years of consecutive growth, dragging down the dividend growth metrics [1]. The 5-year dividend growth CAGR of 2.44% reflects the disruption of the cut. Pro forma organic revenue growth of 6.5% approaches but does not reach the 10% target, while the 5-year revenue CAGR of 2.7% reflects the turbulence of the Worldpay era. Liquidity is excellent at $269M average daily dollar volume.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): FIS's core banking software is deeply embedded in 8,500+ financial institutions across 120+ countries with extremely high switching costs — multi-year contracts, regulatory compliance certifications, and mission-critical transaction processing create formidable barriers to displacement [1]. Banking technology is among the most heavily regulated software categories, requiring SOC 2, PCI-DSS, and bank-specific certifications that take years to obtain. AI-native competitors would need to replicate not just the software but the regulatory trust framework. However, AI could enable new entrants to build modern cloud-native banking stacks more efficiently, potentially threatening FIS's legacy on-premise installations, and FIS must continuously invest to prevent its technology from becoming obsolete relative to AI-first competitors like Thought Machine or Mambu. AI Opportunity (1/1): FIS is actively monetizing AI through customer-facing products — Treasury GPT embeds conversational AI into the Treasury and Risk Manager platform via a Microsoft/OpenAI partnership, and the Insurance Risk Suite AI Assistant provides 24/7 generative AI-powered risk modeling [3]. These are shipping products with measurable customer adoption, not just pilot programs, positioning FIS as an AI enabler within its installed base.

infinite_game.summary: FIS scores low on infinite game criteria primarily because its recent history reflects reactive, finite-game decision-making — the $43B Worldpay acquisition (2019) was a scale-driven bet that destroyed shareholder value, and the subsequent divestiture and restructuring were corrective measures forced by market pressure rather than proactive strategic vision. The Glassdoor-documented culture of frequent RIFs and minimal merit increases suggests a low-trust internal environment inconsistent with Sinek's Circle of Safety. While the competitive dynamics with Fiserv and Jack Henry drive improvement, the overall pattern is a company managing quarterly pressures rather than playing to keep playing.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): FIS's stated purpose of 'Advancing the way the world pays, banks and invests' is a serviceable business description but does not articulate a compelling future state that doesn't yet exist [2]. It describes what FIS does today rather than painting a vision worth sacrificing for. Compare this to a genuine Just Cause — there is no sense of a world made fundamentally better, no inclusive invitation to join a movement. The purpose could apply to any fintech company and provides no strategic differentiation or inspirational pull for employees, customers, or stakeholders.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Glassdoor ratings of 3.4/5 with persistent complaints about 'constant RIFs every three months' and merit increases described as 'non-existent or insultingly small' paint a picture of a low-trust internal environment [4]. Frequent restructuring cycles — from the Worldpay integration to the post-divestiture reorganization — create exactly the kind of organizational instability that Sinek warns erodes the Circle of Safety. Employees operating under constant threat of layoffs are unlikely to take creative risks, collaborate openly, or commit beyond their immediate job requirements. This is not an environment where people feel safe enough to be vulnerable.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): FIS operates in a competitive fintech landscape alongside Fiserv (the largest competitor by market cap), Jack Henry & Associates (community banking focus), and Temenos (international core banking). These rivalries drive continuous technology improvement — Fiserv's Clover platform and Jack Henry's cloud-native initiatives push FIS to accelerate its own modernization. FIS's strategic pivot to pure-play banking/payments tech was partly informed by competitive dynamics, showing the company learns from and responds to rivals rather than simply trying to defeat them.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The Worldpay divestiture and simultaneous Issuer Solutions acquisition fundamentally reshaped FIS from a diversified payments-and-banking conglomerate into a focused banking and issuer technology provider [2]. This was a dramatic strategic pivot involving the sale of a business acquired for $43B just six years earlier. The willingness to reverse course demonstrates flexibility, but the move was driven largely by necessity — the Worldpay acquisition had destroyed shareholder value, and activist pressure was mounting. True existential flexibility in Sinek's framework involves proactive reinvention to advance a Just Cause, not reactive cleanup of a strategic mistake.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): CEO Stephanie Ferris showed corporate courage in executing the Worldpay divestiture — selling what was once positioned as a crown jewel acquisition required acknowledging a $43B strategic error to shareholders and the market [2]. Her $1M personal stock purchase at $50.39 per share in March 2026 demonstrated personal financial conviction in the restructured company [3]. However, the broader pattern suggests courage born of necessity rather than proactive vision — the divestiture came after years of shareholder value destruction, and the frequent RIFs suggest a culture of cost management over investment in people.

investment_thesis: FIS at 6.03x forward earnings with a PEG ratio of 0.46 represents a deeply discounted pure-play banking technology franchise serving 8,500+ financial institutions globally [4]. The January 2026 restructuring — divesting Worldpay and acquiring Issuer Solutions — creates a focused company with Q1 2026 pro forma organic revenue growth of 6.5% and adjusted EBITDA margins of 39.6%, demonstrating improving execution [1]. Adjusted EPS grew 12% YoY to $1.36 in Q1, and the 4.56% dividend yield with a conservative 32.80% payout ratio provides income with ample room for dividend growth. CEO Ferris's $1M personal stock purchase at $50.39 signals insider conviction that the stock is significantly undervalued at current levels [3]. The 'Total Issuing' platform combining legacy FIS and newly acquired Issuer Solutions capabilities positions the company to cross-sell debit, credit, and prepaid processing to its massive installed base. With ROE of 17.22%, profit margins of 23.35%, and mission-critical software embedded in regulated financial institutions, FIS offers a compelling risk/reward at a historically cheap valuation.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on the wreckage of the Worldpay era and execution risk in the post-restructuring integration. The stock has declined 52% over the past year, reflecting deep market skepticism about FIS's ability to execute after the $43B Worldpay acquisition destroyed massive shareholder value and led to billions in goodwill writedowns in 2022-2023 [4]. D/E of 132% remains elevated, and while the Issuer Solutions acquisition adds capabilities, integration risk is significant — combining two large technology platforms while maintaining service quality for thousands of financial institutions is operationally complex. The 5-year revenue CAGR of just 2.7% through the Worldpay turbulence and a dividend that was cut in 2023 (only 2 years of consecutive growth) undermine confidence in capital allocation discipline [2]. Glassdoor ratings of 3.4/5 with complaints about constant layoffs suggest talent retention challenges that could impair integration execution [4]. The banking technology market faces long-term disruption risk from cloud-native competitors like Thought Machine and Temenos, and FIS must invest heavily to modernize its legacy on-premise installed base or risk gradual customer attrition to more modern platforms.
key_catalysts: Issuer Solutions integration creating a 'Total Issuing' platform that unlocks cross-sell opportunities across debit, credit, and prepaid processing for 8,500+ financial institution clients, potentially accelerating organic revenue growth above the current 6.5% pro forma rate [1][2]

AI product monetization through Treasury GPT and Insurance Risk Suite AI Assistant driving incremental revenue within the installed base, with the Microsoft/OpenAI partnership providing enterprise credibility and distribution leverage [3]

Valuation re-rating from 6.03x forward PE as the market gains confidence in post-restructuring execution — the 0.46 PEG ratio implies significant upside if FIS delivers on its growth targets and proves the Worldpay chapter is definitively closed [4]
key_risks: Integration execution risk from combining FIS's legacy banking platform with the newly acquired Issuer Solutions business — technology platform mergers in financial services are notoriously complex, and service disruptions could damage relationships with regulated financial institution clients [2]

Legacy technology debt from on-premise core banking installations faces long-term displacement risk from cloud-native competitors like Thought Machine, Temenos, and Mambu, requiring sustained investment to modernize while maintaining backward compatibility for existing clients

Capital allocation credibility deficit after the $43B Worldpay acquisition and subsequent dividend cut in 2023 — management must demonstrate disciplined execution and consistent returns to rebuild investor trust, and any misstep in the integration will compound the credibility gap [4]
sources: FIS Q1 2026 Earnings Release — $3.30B revenue (+30% GAAP, +6.5% pro forma organic), adjusted EPS $1.36 (+12% YoY), EBITDA margin 39.6%, April 2026
FIS Full-Year 2025 Results and 2026 Outlook — $10.68B revenue (+5.4%), Worldpay divestiture and Issuer Solutions acquisition completed January 2026, February 2026

FIS AI Product Launches — Insurance Risk Suite AI Assistant (February 2026), Treasury GPT with Microsoft/OpenAI partnership, CEO Stephanie Ferris insider purchase of 19,846 shares at $50.39, March 2026
StockAnalysis.com — FIS key statistics, forward PE 6.03, PEG 0.46, ROE 17.22%, Glassdoor 3.4/5 rating, accessed June 2026
